result:
There are mornings when I wake up already convinced that every atom in the universe has drifted half an angstrom to the left, a microscopic exodus that leaves the bedspread feeling like someone else’s and the air tasting faintly metallic, as though every appliance in the building has been quietly bleeding ions while I slept. I used to chalk that up to low blood sugar or a poor mattress, but after years of these unannounced realignments I have decided that the phenomenon deserves its own name: “cosmic jet-lag,” an imperceptible lag between one’s inner cadence and the rotational appetite of the planet. You can’t Google it; it hasn’t an ICD-10 code; yet it’s as corporeal as a paper cut and twice as persistent. And the only remedy I’ve discovered is to sit very still on the fire-escape with a mug of coffee that cooled ten minutes ago, scanning the alley for evidence that squirrels—those kinetic little editors of public space—have relocated the morning while I wasn’t looking.  

We treat the small displacements in our lives as irritants, flecks of disorder to be dabbed away so the big story can lumber forward, but I’m convinced the small ones are the true text and everything else is merely a runic caption. The hangnail, the mispronounced consonant, the thirty-seven cents that vanish between sidewalk cracks—these aren’t footnotes to some grand narrative; they are the narrative, written in a shorthand so old we mistake it for background noise. Borrow a microscope from any high-school lab and point it at the dust motes drifting across a shaft of sunlight, and you’ll be confronted with a metropolis of fragments: pollen grains embossed like medieval coins, fibers flayed from somebody’s bargain sweater, skin cells that once formed the left knuckle of a stranger writing a rent cheque. Each speck is freighted with backstory, but no library wants the archive; archivists prefer presidents and wars, not dandruff.  

I keep a notebook labeled “Minor Apocalypses.” It sounds dramatic, yet the entries are almost comically tiny: the day every elevator in my office building hiccupped and displayed the wrong floor numbers; the Tuesday my neighbor’s wind chimes rearranged themselves into a minor pentatonic scale I’d never heard before; the night the supermarket replaced its fluorescent tubes and suddenly the oranges looked embarrassed, as though caught dreaming of being tangerines. I jot the date, the hour, the weather, and then try to reconstruct the moment before the rupture arrived. The futility of that task is the point. If you can’t locate the seam, you’re forced to concede that perhaps the fabric was always porous, that what we call “ordinary” is simply a truce between catastrophes too small to name.  

I used to teach a night class titled “Writing from the Margins,” though most of my students arrived expecting lessons on how to marginally write. They sat at Formica desks clutching thermoses of soup, ready to discuss theme and marketplace, and instead I asked them to describe the sound of their apartment’s radiator valve at 2 A.M. They looked betrayed, as if I’d promised them a map to El Dorado and handed them a bus ticket to the suburbs. “But who cares about my radiator?” one woman objected. Her fingernails were painted the exact shade of red that budget airlines use to sell discount Caribbean flights. I told her that somewhere, in some other session of cosmic jet-lag, a reader might awake with the metallic taste in their mouth, and if we had done the homework properly, the cadence of her radiator valve might be the only thing tethering that stranger to the spinning world. She grimaced, unconvinced, but submitted a paragraph so visceral I could smell the mineral deposits hissing through the pipes. Years later she emailed me: her brother in Kandahar had read the piece and for the first time forgot to be afraid while waiting for the dawn patrol. She signed off with a radiator emoji. I replied with a squirrel. Some acknowledgments transcend spelling.  

There’s a theory—half cosmology, half living-room grievance—that dark matter is simply all the lost objects pooled together into an invisible gravity. Left socks, toy wheels, passports, wedding rings, the years you meant to give back to your parents but never figured out how—nothing vanishes, it just migrates to this reserve of unavailable mass tugging gently at the orbits of planets. The idea is ridiculous but mathematically comforting: to think that every mislaid item still exists somewhere, adhesive as grief, spinning arm in arm with galaxies. When my mother died, I turned her house upside down seeking a turquoise cuff she’d worn every spring. No luck. A decade later my niece reached into a cereal box at a sleepover in another state and drew out the bracelet amid cornflakes. Box, factory, distribution chain—none had intersected with Mom’s life, but there the cuff was, unwilling to explain the commute. The physicist inside me snorted; the grandson inside me wept; the mammal who eats breakfast simply clasped the jewelry around his niece’s wrist and felt the universe adjust by half an angstrom to the right.  

I mention my mother because she was fluent in the grammar of small catastrophes. When I was eight I spilled grape juice on her white linen tablecloth—an artisanal stain shaped like the Aegean. I braced for thunder, but she studied the atlas of purple, nodded judiciously, and embroidered cobalt waves around it. By the time the cloth emerged from her hoop, there was no trace of mishap, only a mythic sea where tiny goldfish (she used thread filched from an old sari) darted beneath boats stitched from the same indigo that trimmed the spill. We ate Sunday dinner on that ocean for fifteen years. When the cloth finally wore thin, she laundered it, ironed it, and mailed it—without note—to a cousin she hadn’t spoken to since Eisenhower. I asked why. “Because,” she said, setting her teacup down at the precise coordinate where once a purple continent had lurked, “some things are finished when they still look whole.” It was the closest she ever came to admitting that narrative is not a straight avenue but a cul-de-sac that flowers into side streets while nobody’s looking.  

We talk about “finding closure” as if closure were an earring rolled under the dresser, ready to be retrieved with a flashlight and a yardstick. My experience suggests the opposite: closures find us. They arrive in the shape of a stranger’s laughter that happens to echo your dead friend’s, in an advertisement for a medication you no longer need, in the sudden knowledge that you can fold your late lover’s T-shirt without flinching. These pardon coupons surface at illicit hours when your vigilance is dozing, and they never look the way rehearsed speeches do. I once spent six months scripting what I would say to my father’s voicemail if he ever answered—he never did—yet the ache subsided the night a street-busker outside a taco stand played “Mr. Tambourine Man” on a dented clarinet. I don’t even like Dylan, but I tossed five bucks into the open case and walked home lighter by a grief I hadn’t known was portable. The body, it seems, keeps score on erasable ink; the trick is that the eraser is alive and capricious and works the graveyard shift.  

This fluidity of meaning is treasonous to the industries built on permanence: insurance, monument, scripture, museum, law. They require that events solidify, certify, ossify, so that actuaries and judges can perform triage centuries later. Yet lived experience behaves more like the weather forecast for tomorrow—a probability cone, widening into butterfly futures. The smartphone pings with a tornado watch; outside, the sky yawns, unimpressed. One block over, a shed tumbles into a golf course minutes before the alert is downgraded. Who was right, alert or sky? Both; neither; take your pick. I think of that tension whenever I walk past the bronze statue of a general two streets from my apartment. He sits astride a stallion frozen in mid-plunge, sword raised against enemies who died a hundred years before their grandchildren marched for civil rights. The plaque lists battles I memorized in middle school, but now I can only notice that pigeons have painted his epaulettes white, as though the metal itself were surrendering rank, bird by irreverent bird. One day a city crew will scaffold him, scrub him, restore patina, and still the war will be over; the birds will return; the cone of probability will widen.  

It’s tempting, then, to claim that permanence is a myth, but myths are how we breathe. When my best friend got diagnosed with stage-four everything, we compiled an anti-bucket list: experiences he was content to skip—sky-diving, truffle hunting, learning to tango. Instead, we went to the county fair and ate funnel cake dusted with so much powdered sugar we looked like burglars who’d broken into a cocaine warehouse. A kid wearing LED sneakers raced past and screeched, “Mom, those men are aging backwards!” We laughed until we cried, and then kept laughing because crying was also on the anti-bucket list. Somewhere in that white cloud of sugar and dawn-lit terror, permanence re-entered: not in the disease, but in the smell of yeast and burnt oil that will ride my synapses any time I hear carnival music, and yes, that too is a kind of bronze, though it weighs nothing and can’t be stolen by pigeons.  

Memory, apparently, does not possess; it flavors. When the mind sifts through the pantry of years, it isn’t counting jars; it is reading spice levels. A single clove of cinnamon—your toddler biting your shoulder—can over-simmer an entire decade. Conversely, whole civilizations resided in your twenties that left no parsley trace, and you’re forced to trust photographs you don’t recall posing for. I find that reassuring: to be edited by oneself is to be co-authored by time, that meticulous but distracted editor who sometimes rewrites an entire chapter while keeping the original spelling mistakes. The result is a manuscript we keep reading aloud to anyone patient enough, hoping they’ll catch plot holes we’re too enamored to see, and maybe they’ll also notice, tucked between two adjectives, a doodle of a radiator valve that keeps the stranger from drifting off.  

I read once that neurons repurpose taste receptors to help form memories, which explained why a sip of the chalky electrolyte drink I guzzled after childhood fevers still makes me feel forgiven, as though my mother just finished dabbing the cloth with Aegean waves. Biochemistry is the original plagiarist; it steals structures meant for the tongue and converts them into clichés the heart recognizes: chicken soup equals safety, burnt toast equals evacuation, fermented grape equals nearly every human rite. The chain reaction is so fast we call it instinct, but “instinct” is merely hindsight wearing reflective gear, waving from the wreckage. Taste is a faster archive than language, but it’s also a shapeshifter, altering every time a new city adds an accent to the broth. Return to the soup of childhood, and you will taste not the past but your own evolution in salt tolerance, in herb prejudice, in umami sophistication. The tongue is a diary that rewrites itself while you pronounce today’s passwords.  

I’ve wandered far from cosmic jet-lag, but maybe not. After all, what is jet-lag but the mouth reporting a quarrel with the clock? The stomach insists it is time for rice, while the courtyard outside is still assembling its dawn chorus. The body wants its routines authenticated, notarized by sunrise, but the airplane—or the anxiety dream, or the sudden phone call at 3 A.M.—relocates you before your entrails receive the memo. And so you stand barefoot on fire-escape metal at dawn, indexing flavors that don’t yet belong to this longitude, waiting for a squirrel to approve the itinerary. Sometimes the animal arrives, sometimes not, but the city keeps exhaling bus exhaust and bakery steam, constructing the olfactory atmosphere your brain will later file under “phase-shift triggers.” And you forgive the squirrel either way, because mammals understand the calculus of appearing: risk versus curiosity, calories versus cat. It is more honest than most op-eds.  

Honesty, though, is another one of those bronze statues, regularly scrubbed by pigeons. We insist we want it, but we flinch from unfiltered self-interrogation the way we flinch from looking directly at a fluorescent bulb. Years ago I participated in a meditation retreat where the teacher encouraged us to gaze into our own eyes using a handheld mirror for forty-five minutes. Try it; you’ll discover that your face is an unstable parlor trick. First you look heroic, then tragically flawed, then boring, then unexpectedly tender toward the flawed hero you inhabit. Somewhere minute twenty, the boundary between inspection and voyeurism dissolves, and you start to feel like a Peeping Tom trespassing in your own skull. I laughed so hard the mirror fogged, and the teacher materialized beside me, whispering, “Good, now hold that laugh until it reveals its other side.” I wasn’t sure whether he meant enlightenment or despair, but the laugh eventually folded itself into a sob, then into silence, then into something for which there is no bronze, no spice, no dark matter, only the echo of a valve hissing through the pipes of a building I no longer live in. Honesty became the space where laughter and crying exchange coats without checking the pockets.  

We live, then, in layers of partial reportage. The brain edits; the tongue rewrites; the city redacts; the universe drifts. All archives leak. Does that depress you? It exhilarates me, because it means nothing is fixed enough to tyrannize forever. Even granite is on sabbatical courtesy of erosion; even trauma, given solvent and chorus, can be nudged toward a new coastline given enough centuries or coffee or clarinets. Of course, “enough” is unverifiable, the ultimate whittled horizon. But the horizon’s very existence allows us to practice desire, which in turn manufactures tomorrow, which supplies more dust for the microscope, more pigeons for the statue, more missing socks for the dark matter ledger. Desire is the opposite of arrival; it is the bus that departs just as your lungs fill with burnt-funnel-cake smoke, promising that somewhere—possibly the next county, possibly the next sentence—you will exhale and the world will click half an angstrom back toward center.  

I return to the fire-escape. The coffee in my mug is now thoroughly cold, and the alley smells of wet cardboard because Tuesday is recycling day. No squirrels, but a plastic bag has staged an imitation: it jerks upward in a spiral, hesitates, snags on the rusted ladder, collapses. The performance lasts ten seconds, yet I feel I have watched a five-act tragedy compressed by an MFA program. If I note it in “Minor Apocalypses,” who will read it and feel the orbit tug? Maybe no one; maybe you; maybe the dark matter itself, hungry for yet another sock. The notebook will yellow, the ink fade, but the cardboard will compost, the ladder flake into rust powder, and somewhere—perhaps in your kitchen right now—a cereal box waits to surrender a turquoise cuff to a child who will grow up to spill grape juice on an heirloom, wave a crayon, and outsource the next embroidery to time.  

Outside my window the city has finished digesting dawn; buses begin their serious routes, and the day shifts from probability to something stiffer, like responsibility. Cosmic jet-lag levels out, or maybe I merely grow accustomed to the tilt. Either way, I carry the notebook inside, slot it among the other spines that pretend to be books but are actually ballast. They keep the apartment from floating away during especially quiet nights. You’re welcome to borrow any volume; just be advised that the margins have been known to rearrange themselves when nobody’s looking. Words slip half an angstrom, and suddenly a recipe for lentil soup contains a line about forgiveness that tastes like iron, or a tax return sprouts a stanza describing the Aegean in cobalt waves.  

Promise me this: when you notice the air going metallic, step out onto whatever ledge you can find. Sit still until a squirrel—or a plastic bag impersonating one—performs its editorial duties. Pay attention on the order of microscope, tongue, radiator valve. Write it down, speak it, embroider it, or simply taste it very deliberately. The universe is not keeping score; it is keeping cadence. Your part is a single beat, but it aligns neighboring beats in ways that bronze statues and prescription leaflets will never declare. Out of those microscopic allegiances, whole architectural decades will unknowingly be braced. And if you ever unwrap a cereal box and find a turquoise cuff, clasp it around somebody’s wrist before gravity changes its mind.  

That, or laugh until the mirror fogs. Either counts as arrival, both are departure.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
